model.label: DeepSeek V3.2
model.description: DeepSeek • General-purpose V-series model optimized for reasoning, coding, and efficient inference.

pick.rationale: Duke's status as the No. 1 overall seed is backed by the nation's top defense and an elite top-five offense, a combination that should control this Elite Eight matchup. Even with the absence of Caleb Foster and potential limitations for Patrick Ngongba, the Blue Devils' depth and efficiency have proven sufficient in prior tournament rounds. Michigan State's defensive discipline and tournament experience will make this competitive, but Duke's superior overall team quality and balanced attack should secure a hard-fought victory to reach the Final Four.

reasoningStep.title: Elite Eight: East Regional Final Evaluation
reasoningStep.summary: Analyzed Duke's elite efficiency metrics and injury context versus Michigan State's tournament path and defensive profile, concluding Duke's superior balance and defensive prowess provide the decisive edge.
reasoningStep.evidence: Duke is ranked #1 in Torvik and KenPom with a 31-2 record, featuring the nation's top-ranked defense (adjDE 90.4) and the #4 offense. Prior picks noted Duke has advanced without Caleb Foster and with Patrick Ngongba's expected return, demonstrating depth. Michigan State lacks available efficiency ratings but advanced by exploiting an injured UConn team; their recent 90-80 loss to Michigan suggests potential defensive vulnerabilities. ESPN analysis indicated Michigan State would be a 'tough out' but that Duke is the team to beat them in the Elite Eight.

Boston College Eagles 2d Jeff Borzello Joe Lunardi's ultimate guide to men's March Madness 2026 Illustration by ESPN Joe Lunardi Mar 17, 2026, 09:45 AM ET Close Resident college basketball bracketologist for ESPN Contributor to SportsCenter, ESPN Insider Published first public bracket in 1995 Follow on X Multiple Authors Email Print Open Extended Reactions Our men's NCAA tournament preview has evolved since the first was created 30-odd years ago. It's how Bracketology began, when a handful of hoop nerds gathered during "Selection Weekend" to put together an 80-page postseason "Blue Ribbon" guide. It was the ultimate conclusion to spending the college basketball season poring over résumés, watching games and evaluating strengths, weaknesses and possibilities. All on the crew's own time. The guide has shrunk a tad in length and is no longer in print, but it still fulfills the same role: outlining the crucial information you need to fill out brackets. We've made the case for exactly how each team will advance -- and how they won't. Bracketologist in Chief Joe Lunardi also ran the numbers one last time to forecast where each will actually finish in the final field of 68. result.text: Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket - The Athletic Premier League Champions League 2026 Men's World Cup F1 Tennis FA Cup Cricket La Liga Europa League Women's Football Olympics Global Sports Cycling Sailing Sports Business Boxing Golf Serie A Bundesliga International Football NFL NBA MLB World Baseball Classic NHL MLS Transfer News FPL League Cup Championship League One League Two Women's Euros NWSL College Sports NCAAF NCAAM Peak MMA Motorsports NASCAR Culture Memorabilia & Collectibles Gaming Sports Betting The PL Is Being Left Behind Welcome to Chelsea, Liam Rosenior PL Player of the Year Nominees Log In Subscribe Now Tickets by Viagogo Connections: Sports Edition REGION U.S. International Search Top News Podcasts NCAAM Bracket Scores & Schedule Teams Alabama Arizona Arkansas Duke Florida Gonzaga Houston Illinois Iowa State Kansas Louisville Miami (FL) Michigan State Michigan Nebraska North Carolina Purdue Saint Mary's St. John's Tennessee Texas Tech UConn Vanderbilt Virginia Wisconsin Standings Beat Our Expert's Bracket Tournament Projections Printable Bracket Predictions Game Men's March Madness LIVE First Four Updates Tournament Forecast Challenge Our Experts The Perfect Bracket Bracket Busters NBA Prospects to Watch Key Injuries To Know Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket Patrick Ngongba II and Duke are the overall No. 1 seed, but a couple of injuries could make it more vulnerable. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though
